industryRead.buyerProfile: Procurement and operations buyers at cafés, caterers, and distributors — spec-driven (capacity, material, compliance), price-negotiated, reorder-heavy.
industryRead.channelNotes: B2B: orders are quotes, not checkouts. Compliance (FSC, EN 13432 compostable, food-contact) is frequently a hard gate, not a nice-to-have.

claims.text: Faceted spec filtering is the highest-leverage merchandising move for a SKU catalogue — optimized product lists cut abandonment from 67–90% to 17–33%.

sources.title: Baymard — E-Commerce Product List Usability Benchmark

claims.text: B2B buyers complete most of the journey rep-free and spend only ~17% of buying time with suppliers, so self-serve discovery plus RFQ capture intent better than a contact form.
42% of users try to gauge a product's size from its images; without an in-scale visual they hesitate or mis-order.

sources.title: Baymard — Provide at Least One 'In Scale' Image

claims.reviewerNote: Stat re-read directly from the source on 2026-06-11.

claims.text: 54% of e-commerce sites fail to reflect the chosen finish/colour in the product image; swatch switching that re-renders the preview reduces wrong orders and upsells premium finishes.

sources.title: Baymard — Thumbnails Should Update to Match the Variation (54% Don't)

claims.reviewerNote: Stat re-read directly from the source on 2026-06-11.

claims.text: Framing wall-types as good-better-best (single / double / ripple) guides buyers to the middle tier and anchors the premium option; the middle tier is most-chosen.

sources.title: HBR — The Good-Better-Best Approach to Pricing

claims.text: Procurement buyers treat certifications (FSC, EN 13432) as hard gates; making them filterable turns a compliance objection into a funnel.

sources.title: Baymard — Product List Usability Benchmark (filter by decision-driving attributes)

claims.reviewerNote: Supported by general filtering research applied to compliance by extension — no certification-specific study. Verify before putting in front of a client.

recommendations.title: Lead with capacity + material + finish filtering
recommendations.rationale: Your buyers shop by spec, not product name. The derived filter bar collapses 12 families to the few that match in seconds — the McMaster-Carr pattern engineers already trust — and does the qualifying your sales desk does today.

recommendations.title: Frame hot cups as good / better / best
recommendations.rationale: Single-wall (good), double-wall (better), ripple-wall (best) turns a flat grid into a guided decision and anchors the premium ripple wall — most buyers pick the middle, lifting average spec.

recommendations.title: Add in-use context shots to Hot Food Containers
recommendations.rationale: Containers sell on the application — show them filled, on a counter, in a bag. Buyers buy the outcome; a context shot lets them self-qualify for their use case.

recommendations.title: Lean on print-finish swatches to upsell custom print
recommendations.rationale: Showing full-colour print beside plain kraft, live on the cup, is the cheapest upsell you have — and stops buyers ordering the wrong finish.

recommendations.title: Promote FSC / compostable to buyer-facing filters
recommendations.rationale: Compliance is a hard gate in procurement. A 'compostable' filter turns 'do you even have…?' into a one-click path to the compliant range.

recommendations.title: Make the multi-line quote the conversion event
recommendations.rationale: Don't force checkout on negotiated B2B pricing. The quote basket captures a qualified, fully-specified lead instead of a 'please call me'.

exemplars.name: McMaster-Carr
exemplars.why: The reference for parametric filtering on a deep parts catalogue.

exemplars.name: IKEA
exemplars.why: In-scale and in-situ imagery so buyers never guess size or use.

valuePitch.headline: Your PDF can't answer a buyer's first three questions. The web catalogue answers them in ten seconds.
valuePitch.narrative: Verdant's catalogue is a beautiful PDF that makes the buyer work: to find a compostable 12 oz double-wall cup they scroll, squint at a spec table, and email your desk. The interactive catalogue turns that into capacity + material + finish filters (the McMaster-Carr pattern your buyers already trust), to-scale cup lineups, and print-finish swatches that upsell custom print — then captures the order as a multi-line quote, not a dead 'contact us'. Every move below traces to named e-commerce UX and B2B-buying research.

topMoves.why: B2B buyers shop by spec; optimized lists cut abandonment from 67–90% to 17–33%, and self-serve carries ~83% of the journey.
42% of buyers try to judge size from images; showing the family to scale removes the hesitation that loses the order.

topMoves.move: Print-finish swatches that re-render the cup
topMoves.why: 54% of sites don't reflect the chosen finish; doing it prevents wrong orders and upsells premium print.

valuePitch.nextStep: Book a 30-minute call — we'll demo Verdant's own catalogue, live and branded, filtering in real time.
